The amendment ended the legal institution of slavery but did not resolve the underlying racial hierarchy, which reconstructed itself through Jim Crow, convict leasing, and structural racism.

label: Camp David Accords
summary: The 1978 peace agreements between Egypt and Israel, brokered by President Carter at Camp David, establishing a peace framework and Egypt's recognition of Israel — the first Arab country to do so. Egypt regained the Sinai Peninsula; Israel gained normalized relations with its largest Arab neighbor and removal of the largest military threat. Both Sadat and Begin received the Nobel Peace Prize. The accords demonstrated that sustained personal diplomacy between leaders willing to make existential compromises could resolve conflicts where all parties thought peace impossible.

label: Partial Nuclear Test Ban Treaty
summary: The 1963 treaty banning nuclear weapons tests in the atmosphere, underwater, and in space — the first nuclear arms control agreement, signed by the US, USSR, and UK. It came directly from the Cuban Missile Crisis (1962), which had brought the world closest to nuclear war and shocked both superpowers into recognizing that escalation had no floor. The treaty was also driven by scientific evidence of radioactive fallout's health effects. It halted the most dangerous form of nuclear testing and established the precedent for subsequent arms control: SALT, START, NPT.

label: Paris Agreement
summary: The 2015 UN climate accord, signed by 196 countries, committing to limit global warming to well below 2°C above pre-industrial levels. The first universal climate agreement — unlike Kyoto (1997), which covered only developed countries. Nations set their own nationally determined contributions (NDCs) rather than accepting binding caps, making it politically achievable but weaker on enforcement. The agreement's significance is the universal acknowledgment of shared responsibility and the $100 billion per year climate finance commitment to developing nations. Whether its goals are achievable depends on whether commitments are met.

label: Mahatma Gandhi
summary: Mohandas Karamchand Gandhi (1869–1948), leader of India's independence movement and developer of satyagraha — nonviolent resistance as a political philosophy and practice. Tested in South Africa (1893–1914) against racial discrimination laws; deployed at scale in India against British rule through the Non-Cooperation Movement (1920), Salt March (1930), and Quit India Movement (1942). Assassinated January 30, 1948 by a Hindu nationalist. His methods directly inspired Martin Luther King Jr., the civil rights movement, Nelson Mandela (before his ANC period), and virtually every subsequent nonviolent resistance movement.

label: Eleanor Roosevelt
summary: Eleanor Roosevelt (1884–1962), First Lady 1933–1945, later U.S. delegate to the UN and chair of the UN Commission on Human Rights that drafted the Universal Declaration of Human Rights (1948). The most influential First Lady in American history — she transformed the role into an independent political force, advocating for Black civil rights (resigning from the DAR when it refused Marian Anderson), labor rights, and women's equality. Her diplomatic work after FDR's death was arguably her most lasting legacy: the UDHR is her most important achievement.

label: Martin Luther King Jr.
summary: Martin Luther King Jr. (1929–1968), Baptist minister and civil rights leader who led the Montgomery Bus Boycott (1955), the Birmingham Campaign (1963), the March on Washington ('I Have a Dream,' 1963), and the Selma-to-Montgomery marches (1965). Nobel Peace Prize 1964. King synthesized Gandhi's nonviolent resistance with the Black church's prophetic tradition and Thoreau's civil disobedience into the most effective mass movement for racial equality in American history. Assassinated April 4, 1968 in Memphis. His final years criticized the Vietnam War and economic inequality — positions that have been systematically sanitized by the 'safe MLK' myth.

label: César Chávez
summary: César Chávez (1927–1993), co-founder with Dolores Huerta of the United Farm Workers (UFW) in 1962. Led the Delano grape strike and boycott (1965–1970), the first successful farmworker strike in U.S. history, securing a contract from major California growers. Used Gandhian nonviolent tactics — strikes, boycotts, marches, fasts — to organize the most vulnerable agricultural workers: Latino and Filipino migrants without legal protections. Established that nonviolent organizing could build durable labor power for workers most excluded from existing union structures.

label: Dolores Huerta
summary: Dolores Huerta (born 1930), co-founder of the United Farm Workers with César Chávez and the movement's primary organizer and lobbyist. Coined 'Sí, se puede' ('Yes, we can'). Negotiated the landmark 1970 grape contracts; led the national boycott that built consumer pressure on California growers. Less famous than Chávez but arguably equally essential — she handled the political and legislative strategy while Chávez led public actions. A foundational figure in Chicana feminism and in the model of combining labor organizing with civil rights activism.

label: Eight-Hour Day Movement
summary: The campaign to limit the industrial working day to eight hours — 'eight hours for work, eight hours for rest, eight hours for what we will.' The demand emerged from the 1840s; the pivotal moment was the Haymarket affair (Chicago, May 4, 1886), when a bomb thrown at police during a labor rally killed officers and led to the wrongful execution of eight anarchists. May Day (May 1) commemorates Haymarket worldwide. The eight-hour day was won incrementally: Ford Motor Company adopted it voluntarily in 1914 (finding it increased productivity); the Fair Labor Standards Act (1938) made it federal law. It established the principle that workers' time belongs to them, not entirely to employers.

label: Rural Electrification
summary: The Rural Electrification Administration (REA, 1935), part of the New Deal, extended electric power to American farms — only 10% of rural homes had electricity in 1935; by 1950, 90% did. Delivered through rural electric cooperatives (still operating today), electricity transformed farm life: refrigeration, running water, powered tools, and radio ended the isolation and physical hardship of rural existence. LBJ credited the REA with transforming the Texas Hill Country of his childhood from pre-industrial poverty to modern life. It is the canonical example of public infrastructure investment reaching populations that private markets deemed unserved.

label: Simón Bolívar
summary: Simón Bolívar (1783–1830), Venezuelan military and political leader who led the independence movements of Venezuela, Colombia, Ecuador, Peru, and Bolivia from Spanish colonial rule. Called 'El Libertador.' Led multiple campaigns across the Andes under extraordinary conditions; his vision was a unified South American federation — Gran Colombia — which fractured after independence. Bolívar's career demonstrated both the possibility of large-scale anti-colonial liberation and its limits: political unity across post-colonial societies proved harder to achieve than military victory. His legacy is claimed across the political spectrum in Latin America.

label: Emma Goldman
summary: Emma Goldman (1869–1940), anarchist political activist and writer who advocated for labor rights, women's emancipation, free speech, and birth control. Emigrated from Russia to the U.S.; organized workers and spoke on anarchist philosophy across the country. Deported to Russia in 1919 after opposing WWI conscription. Her writings ('Anarchism and Other Essays,' 1910) articulated a vision of freedom from state and capitalist domination simultaneously — an integration of political, economic, and personal liberation. Influenced the feminist, labor, and free speech movements; her life demonstrated the state's use of deportation as a tool to suppress radical political organizing.

label: Rohingya Genocide
summary: The systematic ethnic cleansing and genocide of the Rohingya Muslim minority in Myanmar by the military (Tatmadaw) and nationalist Buddhist mobs, reaching its most intense phase in August 2017 when 700,000+ Rohingya fled to Bangladesh in three months. UN fact-finders described the campaign as bearing the hallmarks of genocide: mass killings, sexual violence, and burning of villages. Myanmar denied Rohingya citizenship since 1982, rendering them stateless. Facebook's algorithmic amplification of anti-Rohingya hate speech was directly implicated in inciting violence — a documented case of social media enabling genocide. The ICJ (International Court of Justice) ordered Myanmar in 2020 to protect the Rohingya.

label: Long COVID
summary: Persistent symptoms lasting weeks, months, or years after acute COVID-19 infection — affecting an estimated 10–30% of those infected. Symptoms include fatigue, cognitive impairment ('brain fog'), shortness of breath, and post-exertional malaise resembling ME/CFS. By 2023, an estimated 15–20 million Americans had some form of long COVID, representing a significant cause of workforce disability. Long COVID revealed gaps in medical understanding of post-viral syndromes and exposed how quickly a new disease can generate novel disability at population scale. It also surfaced prior neglect of ME/CFS, a condition with similar features that had been underfunded for decades.

label: Defund the Police Debate
summary: The political debate, catalyzed by George Floyd's murder and the 2020 BLM protests, over reallocating police funding to social services, mental health response, housing, and community programs. 'Defund' encompassed positions from abolition to modest reallocation. Minneapolis and other cities made cuts; most reversed them after crime increases. The debate surfaced the question of what police are actually for and whether policing is the appropriate response to problems rooted in poverty, mental illness, and housing instability. The phrase 'defund' became a liability for Democrats in the 2020 elections despite the underlying policy argument being broadly popular when described concretely.

label: Geoffrey Chaucer
summary: English poet (c.1343-1400) who wrote The Canterbury Tales, the first major literary work in vernacular Middle English — establishing English as a literary language and satirizing medieval class and religious hypocrisy.

label: Rosa Luxemburg
summary: German-Polish Marxist revolutionary (1871-1919) who developed spontaneous mass action theory, opposed both Leninist vanguardism and reformist socialism, and was murdered by right-wing Freikorps soldiers after the failed Spartacist uprising.
